title: Perfect Pavlova
ast: Pavlova is one of those rare dishes with a genuine international identity crisis, and both Australia and New Zealand claim it with fierce national pride. The dessert is named after the Russian ballerina Anna Pavlova, who toured both countries in the 1920s. New Zealand food historians point to a 1927 recipe from a Wellington hotel chef; Australians counter with a 1935 cookbook entry. The truth is probably that both countries developed similar meringue-based desserts around the same time, inspired by the ballerina's tutu. What matters is that both nations perfected it, and the world is better for it.

What distinguishes pavlova from a plain meringue is its texture: crisp and papery on the outside, soft and marshmallow-like within. This is achieved through the addition of cornstarch (cornflour) and a touch of vinegar, which stabilize the egg white foam and prevent the interior from setting completely.
Serves 8-10. This recipe makes one 23cm (9-inch) round pavlova.
6 large egg whites, at room temperature
1 teaspoon white wine vinegar (or apple cider vinegar)
250g (about 1 pint) fresh strawberries, hulled and halved or quartered
Optional: fresh raspberries, blueberries, mango slices
Stand mixer or hand mixer with a very clean, grease-free bowl and whisk
Baking sheet lined with parchment paper
Pencil (to trace a circle guide on the parchment)
Offset spatula or the back of a large spoon
If possible, make the meringue base the day before serving. This gives it time to cool completely and dry out in the oven overnight, which produces the best texture.

Take your eggs out of the refrigerator 30-60 minutes before you begin. Room temperature egg whites whip to a greater volume than cold ones. This is not optional — cold whites will work, but you will get a denser meringue.
Preheat your oven to 150°C (300°F). Do not use fan/convection if you can help it — the moving air can cause cracking and uneven drying. Conventional (bottom/top heat) is ideal.
Line your baking sheet with parchment paper. Trace a 23cm (9-inch) circle on the parchment using a plate or cake tin as a guide — draw on the underside so the pencil does not touch the meringue.

Separate your eggs one at a time into a small bowl before adding each white to the main bowl. A single drop of yolk in your whites will prevent them from whipping properly. If a yolk breaks, set that egg aside for another use and start fresh. The small bowl method ensures one accident does not ruin the whole batch.
Your bowl and whisk must be completely free of grease. Wipe them with a paper towel dampened with white vinegar or lemon juice to be sure.

Add the pinch of salt to the egg whites in the bowl of your stand mixer (or a large deep bowl if using a hand mixer). Beat on medium speed until the whites reach soft peaks — this takes about 3-4 minutes. Soft peaks mean the whites hold their shape briefly but the tip flops over when you lift the whisk.

With the mixer running on medium-high, add the caster sugar one tablespoon at a time. Go slowly — adding it all at once can deflate the foam and cause the meringue to weep. Each addition should take about 15-20 seconds to incorporate. The total sugar addition takes 6-8 minutes.

Once all sugar is added, increase to high speed and beat until the meringue is thick, glossy, and holds very stiff peaks. To test: rub a small amount between your fingers. If you can feel any grittiness, the sugar has not fully dissolved — keep beating for another 2-3 minutes and test again. Fully dissolved sugar is critical; undissolved sugar causes weeping. The meringue should look like thick shaving cream and be bright white.

Sift the cornstarch over the meringue. Add the vinegar and vanilla. Fold these in gently using a large spatula with as few strokes as possible — no more than 8-10 folds. You want to preserve as much air as possible. Stop folding as soon as no streaks remain.
Scrape the meringue onto the center of your traced circle. Use the offset spatula or the back of a large spoon to spread it to the edges of the circle.

Shape the edges slightly higher than the center, creating a shallow well or nest in the middle. This hollow will hold the cream and fruit. The sides should be gently swirled or swooped — do not smooth them completely flat; texture helps the meringue dry evenly.
Place in the preheated oven. Immediately reduce the temperature to 120°C (250°F). Bake for 1 hour and 15 minutes to 1 hour and 30 minutes.

What to look for: The surface should be dry and papery to a light touch. It should have a very pale cream or slightly ivory color — not white, not golden, not browned. If it is coloring too quickly, reduce the temperature by 10°C (20°F). It should not crack across the top during baking; gentle cracks on the sides are normal.

Turn the oven off. Do not open the oven door. Leave the pavlova inside as the oven cools completely — ideally overnight, or for at least 2-3 hours. This slow cooling is essential. Rapid temperature change causes the meringue to collapse and crack severely.

Whip the cold heavy cream with the icing sugar and vanilla to soft peaks. Soft, not stiff — you want it to billow and drape, not stand up in stiff peaks, which would make it look heavy and can turn grainy quickly.
Peel the pavlova off the parchment carefully. It will likely have cracked somewhere on the exterior — this is fine and expected. Place on your serving plate or stand.
Spread the whipped cream generously into the hollow and over the top, leaving a border of meringue visible around the edges.
Arrange the fruit over the cream. Passionfruit pulp should be spooned over last as a glossy, tart finish. Serve immediately — once topped, the pavlova will soften within 1-2 hours.

Meringue is hygroscopic — it absorbs moisture from the air. Do not attempt to make pavlova on a rainy or very humid day if you can help it. High humidity causes the sugar to absorb atmospheric moisture during baking and cooling, resulting in a sticky, weeping meringue. If you must bake on a humid day, extend the oven drying time and keep the cooled meringue tightly wrapped.
Undissolved sugar is the most common cause of a weeping pavlova (liquid pooling under the meringue). Add the sugar slowly. Test with your fingers. Do not rush this step.
Fat prevents egg whites from whipping. Any trace of grease — from hands, from a washed-but-not-dried bowl, from a yolk — will sabotage your meringue. Clean everything with vinegar.
Once stiff peaks are achieved, stop. Overbeaten meringue looks grainy and curdled, loses its sheen, and does not hold its shape when baked. It will crack and collapse.
Cold egg whites can whip, but they take longer and produce less volume. Room temperature whites whip up faster and create a more stable, voluminous foam.

ast.raw-value: The Vinegar and Cornstarch Are Not Optional
title: The Vinegar and Cornstarch Are Not Optional

ast: These two ingredients are what make a pavlova different from a meringue. The acid (vinegar) stabilizes the foam and contributes to the soft center. The cornstarch absorbs moisture and creates that characteristic marshmallow interior. Skip them and you will have a crunchy meringue all the way through.

The pavlova will always crack to some degree — this is normal and part of its rustic charm. But severe collapse comes from cooling too fast. Never open the oven door during baking or the first hour of cooling.

ast.raw-value: Common Mistakes and How to Avoid Them
title: Common Mistakes and How to Avoid Them

ast: Add sugar slower, test for grittiness, avoid humid days
Extend oven drying, store in a dry place
Oven too hot, or cooled too fast
Reduce temperature, never open oven door early
Grease in bowl, yolk contamination, cold eggs
Degrease everything, room temp eggs, careful separation
Reduce to 120 degrees C / 250 degrees F, use oven thermometer

The unfilled meringue base can be stored at room temperature in an airtight container or wrapped loosely in plastic wrap for up to 2 days. Do not refrigerate the bare meringue — the refrigerator is humid and will cause it to soften.

Once topped with cream and fruit, serve within 1-2 hours. The cream causes the meringue to soften fairly quickly. You can assemble the cream up to 2 hours ahead if you must, but add the fruit at the last moment — especially the passionfruit, which will cause the cream to weep if left too long.

Leftover topped pavlova can be refrigerated and eaten the next day — it will be soft and collapsed, but many people love this slouchy, cream-soaked version. It tastes wonderful even if it looks a mess.
Serve at room temperature, never cold (it will feel damp and dense straight from the fridge)
A drizzle of honey over the fruit adds a lovely gloss
Fresh mint leaves make a simple, elegant garnish
For a dinner party, assemble at the table for drama
Pairs beautifully with a glass of Champagne, Moscato, or a late-harvest Riesling

Pipe or spoon the meringue into 8-10 individual nests, each about 8cm (3 inches) across. Reduce baking time to 45-55 minutes at 120°C (250°F), then cool in the oven as usual. Individual pavlovas are easier to serve at a dinner party and look elegant. Top each one just before serving.

Spread the meringue onto a parchment-lined 30x40cm (12x16 inch) baking sheet to about 1.5cm (half inch) thick. Bake at 160°C (325°F) for 20 minutes — it should be set on top but still soft inside. Remove, cover with a clean tea towel, and cool completely. Flip onto a fresh sheet of parchment. Spread with whipped cream and fruit, then roll up using the parchment to guide you (expect cracks — embrace them). Refrigerate for 30 minutes before slicing.

Fold 2 tablespoons of sifted good-quality cocoa powder and 50g (2oz) finely chopped dark chocolate into the finished meringue before shaping. Top with cream and raspberries, with a drizzle of ganache.

Replace half the whipped cream topping with good-quality lemon curd, swirled together for a sharp contrast. Top with fresh raspberries and a little lemon zest. This version cuts the sweetness beautifully.
Top with mango, pineapple, and passionfruit. Add a tablespoon of lime zest to the whipped cream and finish with toasted coconut flakes.

Substitute half the caster sugar with light brown sugar for a deeper, caramel-like flavor. The meringue will be beige rather than white. Particularly good with stone fruit (peaches, nectarines) and a drizzle of caramel sauce.
Top with whipped cream, then layer with thin slices of poached quince or pear, toasted hazelnuts, and a drizzle of dark chocolate. A warming alternative when summer fruit is not available.

Caster sugar (superfine sugar) is important — granulated sugar has larger crystals that take longer to dissolve. If you only have granulated, pulse it in a food processor for 30 seconds to make it finer.
An oven thermometer is genuinely useful here — domestic ovens are often 10-20 degrees C off from the dial. Too hot an oven is the most common reason pavlovas brown, crack excessively, or collapse.
The pavlova will shrink slightly as it cools. This is normal.
Passionfruit is the traditional finishing touch and its tartness is essential for balance — do not skip it if you can find it.
